hinge: For a patient missing #21 who does not want extensive treatment, a Maryland bridge (resin-bonded fixed partial denture) is the most conservative fixed option as it requires minimal tooth preparation. Cantilever and 3-unit FPD require more extensive abutment preparation.

A discolored vital lateral incisor (#12) can be managed with a porcelain veneer for esthetic masking without the tooth reduction needed for a full crown. Internal bleaching is for non-vital teeth; a crown is overly aggressive for a vital tooth with only color concerns.

Feldspathic porcelain has the highest glass content among ceramics, providing excellent translucency and esthetics but lower strength. Lithium disilicate and leucite-reinforced ceramics have crystalline reinforcement with less glass phase.

This item appears garbled. A wide and short root in an endodontically treated premolar with full coverage crown is an anatomical risk factor for VRF, but poor quality RCT (inadequately cleaned and obturated) is the more common causative factor. Without the image, this is uncertain.

Replacing #14 and #15 with abutments on #16 and #13 requires a 5-unit FPD (13-14-15-16), which exceeds ideal span. A non-rigid connector between the pontics allows some stress-breaking to accommodate the long span and differential movement between the anterior and posterior abutments.

For full mouth rehabilitation, canine guidance (mutually protected occlusion) is the preferred occlusal scheme as it provides immediate posterior disclusion in lateral excursions, protecting posterior restorations from destructive lateral forces while allowing anterior guidance.

Pain 1 week after crown placement suggests irreversible pulpitis from preparation trauma or open margins. Removing the crown allows access for endodontic treatment if needed. However, this is aggressive — a desensitizing agent or evaluation for reversible causes should precede removal.

Torque/rocking during try-in of a 3-unit FPD indicates a distorted or misfitting framework. Sectioning the FPD allows each retainer to be evaluated individually. The distorted segment can then be soldered after individual seating confirms fit, rather than remaking the entire bridge.

The ridge lap (saddle) pontic design contacts the ridge over a broad convex surface, making it the most difficult to clean due to large tissue contact area and inaccessibility of the concave fitting surface. Ovate and modified ridge lap designs are more hygienic.

Ceramic crown preparation requires 2 mm incisal reduction on functional cusps and 1.5 mm on non-functional cusps to provide adequate bulk for esthetics and strength while preventing fracture under occlusal load.

Open margins both mesially and distally on a crown suggest casting shrinkage during solidification of the metal — the casting contracted away from the die in all dimensions. Over-tapered preparation would show one open margin side; impression distortion would show asymmetrical defects.

For lithium disilicate cementation, the first step is etching the internal surface of the restoration with hydrofluoric acid (5-9.6% for 20 seconds) to create microporosities for micromechanical bonding with resin cement. Sandblasting may follow; phosphoric acid is for tooth preparation, not the ceramic.

After RCT through a conservative MO access on #36 (mandibular first molar), a composite core bonded to remaining tooth structure provides the best combination of retention and tooth preservation. A cast post and core requires more removal of radicular dentin and is more invasive.

To identify a mandibular central incisor (#32), the key feature is that the incisal slopes are inclined distally — the distal half of the incisal edge slopes more toward the distal than the mesial half. This is a distinguishing feature of mandibular centrals versus laterals.

The minimum distance from the restorative margin to the alveolar bone crest is 3-4 mm to preserve biologic width (2 mm junctional epithelium + connective tissue attachment) and allow a 1-2 mm sulcus depth without inflammation or recession.

For full mouth rehabilitation with loss of all posterior teeth (no MIP), the occlusion must be rebuilt in centric relation — the most repeatable, physiologic condylar position. MIP cannot be used as a reference when posterior teeth are destroyed or missing.

Bubbles at the cervical region of a retainer with water and occlusal pressure indicate an open margin between the retainer and abutment — fluid is forced into the gap and air escapes as bubbles. Loose retainer would show movement; connector fracture would show segment mobility.

For a lingually extended laminate veneer, the contact area should be placed one-fourth of the way up the lingual surface and at least 1 mm away from the centric contact area to avoid placing the ceramic-cement interface under direct occlusal loading, which would risk debonding or fracture.

An FPD with open margins and negative ledges causing roughness and floss breakage is clinically unacceptable and cannot be corrected chairside or in the lab. The entire FPD must be remade with proper marginal adaptation to ensure periodontal health and longevity.

Failure to perform two-plane reduction results in an over-prepared tooth, creating excessive space for porcelain. This leaves an abnormally thick, unsupported layer of porcelain on the buccal surface, which is poorly supported by the metal framework and fractures under occlusal load.

Eccentric interference in a child's anterior teeth should be managed by reducing the lingual inclines of the maxillary anterior teeth (per the BULL rule: Buccal of Upper, Lingual of Lower) to eliminate the premature contact during eccentric movements.

Closure from rest position into MIP with an anterior-superior slide indicates a centric relation to MIP discrepancy from a premature contact deflecting the mandible forward. The interference occurs during the closing arc into centric occlusion, not during eccentric movement from MIP.

When removing a crown reveals a discolored GI core with poor margins but good tooth condition and no symptoms, the core should be replaced with a new composite core for better adhesion and esthetics before fabricating a new crown. However, this item is poorly structured with ambiguously ordered options.

Missing #17, #15, #14 with supraerupted #16 and limited space for #15/#14 requires orthodontic treatment first to intrude #16 and create adequate restorative space before implant placement or fixed restoration. Orthodontic correction is the most ideal option to correct the supraeruption and limited space.

Missing 35-38 (Kennedy Class II) requires a direct retainer (clasp) on the most distal abutment (#34 — or #43 is contralateral). The description seems confused, but the intended answer is a direct retainer on the distal abutment of the partial denture framework. Among options, 43 is the listed choice. This item is poorly formed.

For Kennedy Class II no modification with a high buccal frenum on tooth #24 (#24 = maxillary left first premolar), RPI has a distobuccal undercut and mesial rest — the I-bar approach arm would be in the area of the high frenum. RPA (Akers with mesial rest) avoids the frenum area and provides adequate retention.

Horizontal/lateral forces are the most destructive to abutment teeth as they create tipping moments and torque, which periodontal fibers are least adapted to resist. Vertical forces directed along the long axis of the tooth are distributed through the PDL and well tolerated.

A traumatic ulcer from a complete denture pressure point develops within 1-2 days of wearing the prosthesis as the sharp edge or overextended flange compresses the mucosa, causing ischemia and ulceration.

In skeletal Class II patients (retruded mandible), setting the maxillary posterior teeth slightly palatal helps compensate for the skeletal discrepancy and creates a more favorable buccal overjet for function and esthetics.

A pier abutment (#25) with missing #24,26,27 and an MOD amalgam with recession and Grade I mobility requires a survey crown to provide proper contours, guide planes, and rest seats for optimal RPD support and retention. Rest seats alone are insufficient for a compromised pier abutment.
